results.text:
Can a Startup Save Money by Building Its Own CRM? | Analytics India Magazine

For Atonom, the new-vibe-coded CRM reduced annual software costs to $1,200, including hosting, while eliminating the need for a dedicated CRM administrator.

## Key Takeaways

- Atonom replaced a $40,000 Salesforce contract with a $1,200 CRM built using Lovable's platform.
- Leadership questioned Salesforce costs, prompting a shift to a more cost-effective internal solution.
- Alternative CRMs like HubSpot and Pipedrive didn't meet Atonom's needs or budget constraints.
- The custom CRM eliminated the need for a dedicated administrator while improving operational efficiency.
- Atonom's quick prototyping led to rapid adoption of the new CRM by the sales team.

## The vector database invoice that made our CFO scream

Last September, I got called into an “urgent meeting” with our CFO. She slid a spreadsheet across the table. One line item made my stomach drop: Pinecone: $8,200/month.

Our AI-powered document search was costing us nearly $100K annually. And we were barely scratching the surface of what we wanted to build.

“Can we make this cheaper?” she asked.

I spent the next 72 hours

results.text:
Startup Cuts Costs by Building Internal CRM with AI Tools Instead of Salesforce, TechGig

- Software & DevOps
- 3 min read

# A startup replaced Salesforce with a $1,200 internal CRM built using AI tools

A startup named Atonom replaced its costly Salesforce CRM with an internally built system using an AI platform. This move significantly cut annual expenses from $40,000 to $1,200. The company now builds other internal tools, demonstrating a trend where AI makes custom software cheaper than subscriptions. This shift offers substantial savings for small teams.

- Published On Mar 13, 2026 at 08:42 AM IST

For many startups, CRM software becomes a necessary but expensive piece of infrastructure. One small team decided to question that assumption. Startup Atonom replaced its $40,000 per year Salesforce contract with an internally built CRM created using the AI-powered development platform L

results.title: How we replaced 3000 EUR of monthly SaaS with one custom platform for our nonprofit | Mari Luukkainen
results.author: Mari Luukkainen
results.text:
How we replaced 3000 EUR of monthly SaaS with one custom platform for our nonprofit | Mari Luukkainen

# How we replaced 3000 EUR of monthly SaaS with one custom platform for our nonprofit

April 4, 2026

At some point last year we looked at the monthly SaaS bill for Herizon, the talent matching program for immigrant women in Finland that I run, and realized we were paying close to 3,000 EUR a month just for basic operations. Not for anything fancy, just to send emails, manage contacts, run a community, collect applications, and automate a few workflows between all of those.

We had Customer.io for behavioral emails, Mailchimp for newsletters, Ghost for publishing, Mighty Networks for the community, Tally for intake forms, Zapier for gluing things together, Buffer for social media scheduling, and a CRM that didn't really fit but was the least bad option. Eight tools, eight logins, eight

GHL Exit Sovereign Stack

# We paid GHL $13,200 in 2025. I just cancelled.

March 16, 2026 · Updated April 24, 2026

8 min read

AA

Want the actual scripts — not just the architecture?

AI Avengers Lab members get the full working setup: pre-built code, campaign scripts, and weekly live builds. $89/mo.

See what's inside the Lab

March 2026. I am looking at our GHL invoice. $1,100.

That is $13,200 a year. For a CRM that owns our data, locks us into their ecosystem, and charges more every time we grow.

So we built our own.

## What we built

This is version one. We are building this in public, documenting every decision, every mistake, every win.

Here is what we actually use:

CRM: Jarvis (built on OpenClaw + DuckDB). Free. Local. We own everything.

CMS: Sanity. Pay-per-usage. We pay for what we use, not a monthly seat.

App Builds

# How We Replaced 8 SaaS Subscriptions with One Custom Dashboard

2026-04-19·5 min read·Jordan Patterson

A Charlotte client called us in February. Their monthly software bill had crept past $1,840 — across eight different SaaS tools that were supposed to "make things easier." Half of them barely got opened. Two of them did almost the same thing. And the team still ended up exporting CSVs and stitching reports together in Google Sheets every Monday morning.

So we built them one dashboard. One login. One source of truth. Here's exactly how it went down — the tools we replaced, the timeline, and the result.

## The Stack They Were Drowning In

results.title: How I replaced $2,000/month in SaaS with a $200 Claude Code subscription.
results.author: Max Votek
results.text:
How I replaced $2,000/month in SaaS with a $200 Claude Code subscription.

# Max Votek

SubscribeSign in

# How I replaced $2,000/month in SaaS with a $200 Claude Code subscription.

Mar 23, 2026

11

6

4

Share

I built a personal CRM from scratch.

Not a prototype. Not a demo. A production system I use every day.

47 features. 13 AI tools. A vector knowledge base. LinkedIn profile enrichment. A Telegram bot as the primary interface. Automated follow-up logic. Voice memo integration. Email parsing. Trip-based contact suggestions.

It replaced my entire stack: HubSpot, Monday.com, Notion, and Zapier.

Cost: $200/month for Claude Code.

I asked Claude Code to estimate what this would cost a traditional development team.

The answer: 516 hours. $66,400. Four months. Five people: PM, PO, two developers, QA.

I did it in roughly 20 hours across several sessions.

That’s a massive cost reduc

results.title: I was paying $600/mo for 5 SaaS tools. So I built one that does all of them — and open-sourced it. - DEV Community
results.text:
I was paying $600/mo for 5 SaaS tools. So I built one that does all of them — and open-sourced it. - DEV Community

Last year I was running a 12-person team and paying for:

- 📋 Trello — task management ($12/user/mo)
- 📝 Notion — knowledge base ($10/user/mo)
- ⚡ Zapier — workflow automation ($30/mo)
- 📧 Gmail — email (free, but 5 tabs of context switching)
- 🤖 Some chatbot tool — AI assistant ($50/mo)

Total: ~$600/month. Five tools. Five bills. Five places where information went to die. 😩

I kept thinking: why can't one thing do all of this? 🤔

So I built HiveOps🐝 — a self-hosted company operating system with AI agents that actually execute tasks, not just chat.

And I open-sourced it. 🎉

---

## 🛠 What HiveOps Actually Does

It's a full-stack Node.js app that gives your team:

📋 Task Management — priority levels, status tracking, assign to users or AI agents, comments, file a

results.title: How to Replace Salesforce, Zoho, and the Rest with Claude Code — Claude Bootcamp
results.text:
How to Replace Salesforce, Zoho, and the Rest with Claude Code — Claude Bootcamp

EN ES

A client called me in October. Their Salesforce admin had quit three weeks earlier. The license renewal was in 60 days — $147,000 for 22 seats. No one on staff could describe what half the custom fields did. The automation rules were a 14-page PDF that nobody had opened since 2022. The sales VP said, with a straight face, "Just renew it. We'll deal with it later." That's how SaaS incumbents win. Inertia, opacity, and the quiet fear that touching it will break something.

We did not renew it. We replaced it. Here is the playbook — export, rebuild, cutover — and the honest version of what goes wrong.

## What enterprise CRMs actually do (stripped of the mystique)

Salesforce, Zoho, HubSpot Enterprise, and Dynamics all do five things. Everything else is packaging. Once you name the five, the replacement

results.title: I Built an AI Ops Assistant That Replaced $2,400/month in SaaS — In 3 Days, for Under $100/month - DEV Community
results.text:
I Built an AI Ops Assistant That Replaced $2,400/month in SaaS — In 3 Days, for Under $100/month - DEV Community

Last month, one of our engineers typed this into Slack:

/incident what would cause basic authentication to fail even with a properly formed authentication header when the user is present and the roles are correct

Nine seconds later — before anyone else had even read the message — Harper Eye posted a full incident analysis in the thread. It had identified the root cause (a misconfigured`authorizeLocal` setting in`harperdb-config.yaml`), cited three internal sources (our documentation, codebase, and Slack history), listed specific remediation steps, and @-mentioned the five engineers with the most expertise in authentication — by name, with clickable Slack handles.

No one had to context-switch. No one had to dig through four different dashboards. No one had to remember which
